reasoning: Lebanon formed a new reform-minded government in February 2025 under PM Nawaf Salam and President Joseph Aoun. Following the end of the conflict in late 2024, the country is in critical need of reconstruction funds. Parliament recently passed a long-awaited banking secrecy law in April 2025, meeting a major IMF prerequisite. As of May 30, 2025, formal negotiations between the Lebanese government and the IMF have officially resumed, with an IMF delegation in Beirut until June 5. The Finance Ministry explicitly stated its intention to reach a preliminary staff-level agreement (SLA) during this visit. While an SLA often meets the criteria of a "signed" preliminary agreement for financial assistance, Lebanon's deep-rooted political gridlock and pushback from the banking sector regarding restructuring laws pose significant risks of delay. Given that there is only one month until the July 1 deadline, the outcome hinges heavily on the success of the current mission. The active negotiations provide a very strong catalyst, but the tight timeline and historical precedents of Lebanese reform delays make the probability nearly a coin toss.
